text: A forty-five-year-old lady with no history of previous abdominal surgery presented with weight loss, haematuria and abdominal pain. She had no family history of colorectal cancer or inflammatory bowel disease. CT colonogram demonstrated Crohn's disease of the terminal ileum with fistulation to the urinary bladder, sigmoid colon and left salfinx. Extentive disease was further assessed with MRI enterogram which identified minimal additional disease. Cystoscopy confirmed the presence of fistulation to the urinary bladder.

A forty-five-year-old lady with no history of previous abdominal surgery presented with weight loss, haematuria and abdominal pain. She had no family history of colorectal cancer or inflammatory bowel disease. CT colonogram demonstrated Crohn's disease of the terminal ileum with fistulation to the urinary bladder, sigmoid colon and left salfinx. Extentive disease was further assessed with MRI enterogram which identified minimal additional disease. Cystoscopy confirmed the presence of fistulation to the urinary bladder.

The patient is positioned in the Lloyd Davies position, and pneumoperitoneum is established using an open Hasson technique with a 12mm port. Laparoscopic visualization confirms terminal ileitis involving the urinary bladder and sigmoid colon. The surgeon performs a medial to lateral approach, incising the peritoneum below the ileocolic pedicle and developing the retrocholic plane while carefully protecting the duodenum.

The patient is positioned in the Lloyd Davies position, and pneumoperitoneum is established using an open Hasson technique with a 12mm port. Laparoscopic visualization confirms terminal ileitis with involvement of the urinary bladder and sigmoid colon. The surgeon performs a medial to lateral approach, incising the peritoneum below the ileocolic pedicle and developing the retrocholic plane while carefully protecting the duodenum.

The surgeon isolates and divides the ileocolic vessels, extending the dissection of the retrocolic plane cranially. The dissection proceeds from above, identifying the previous planar dissection to facilitate the complete release of the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon isolates and divides the ileocolic vessels, extending the dissection of the retrocolic plane cranially. The dissection proceeds from above, identifying the previous planar dissection to facilitate the complete release of the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and gonadal vessels, then divides the lateral attachments of the cecum. Blunt dissection is used to carefully separate the inflammatory phlegmon, which is adherent to the urinary bladder and left salpinx, avoiding diathermy injuries to the sigmoid colon and left ureter. The terminal ileum is dissected off the urinary bladder and adnexa.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and gonadal vessels, then divides the lateral attachments of the cecum. Blunt dissection is used to carefully separate the inflammatory phlegmon, which is adherent to the urinary bladder and left salpinx, avoiding secondary diathermy injuries to the sigmoid colon and left ureter. The terminal ileum is dissected off the urinary bladder and adnexa.

The surgeon separates an appendix epiploica from the inflammatory phlegmon. The bladder is inspected, showing no visible defects. Methylene blue is injected through the urinary catheter to pressurize the bladder, confirming no extravasation of contrast. A flexible sigmoidoscopy is performed before exteriorizing the specimen.

The surgeon separates an appendix epiploica from the inflammatory phlegmon. The urinary bladder is inspected, revealing no visible defects. Methylene blue is injected through the urinary catheter to pressurize the bladder, with no extravasation of contrast observed.

The surgeon performs on-table flexible sigmoidoscopy, revealing no colonic defects. The pelvis is flooded with normal saline wash, and no gas leak is observed. The umbilical trochar incision is enlarged to five centimeters, and a wound protector is inserted. The specimen is exteriorized and resected, followed by the creation of a double-layer side-to-side hand-sewn anastomosis using resorbable monofilament suture.

Endoscopic examination reveals no colonic defect. The pelvis is flooded with normal saline wash, showing no leak of insufflated gas. The umbilical trocar incision is enlarged to five centimeters, and a wound protector is inserted before exteriorizing and resecting the specimen. A double-layer side-to-side hand-sewn anastomosis is created using resorbable monofilament suture.
The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged home on post-operative day five.
